content:
`runtime: bun` shipped in Pulumi 3.227. We skipped Node entirely. The surprise wasn’t TypeScript — it was state lock contention at 2 AM.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Pulumi announced Bun as a full runtime in 3.227.0 — not just package manager, the whole program executes under Bun. Our greenfield internal platform stack was the guinea pig: VPC, EKS add-ons, RDS, S3 buckets for artifacts, IAM that nobody reads until an audit.
We chose Bun because the team lives in TypeScript, hates waiting on npm ci, and already used Bun locally for scripts. Pulumi.yaml got runtime: bun. No Node in the container. No ts-node. One binary in CI.
First pulumi up on a fresh stack took 23 minutes. Cold start wasn't the bottleneck. State lock contention with a stale CI job was.
Why we tried Bun as runtime
The pitch internally:

• Native TypeScript — no compile step in the pipeline

• bun install on a cold cache: ~38 seconds vs ~2m 10s with npm on the same lockfile

• Single toolchain — fewer “works on my laptop” arguments
The stack was greenfield. No legacy runtime: nodejs baggage. If Bun broke, we lost a Tuesday, not a quarter.
CI image: pulumi/pulumi-bun equivalent pattern — Bun base, Pulumi CLI, our repo. Runner: 4 vCPU, 8GB RAM, GitHub-hosted.
What the first week taught us
Day 1 — happy path

• pulumi preview on empty stack: ~90 seconds

• First pulumi up (VPC + IAM skeleton): ~11 minutes

• Developer feedback: “this feels faster” — mostly install + startup
Day 3 — first real pain
Parallel CI jobs on the same stack — one from a PR preview, one from a manual apply someone kicked off after standup. State backend: S3 + DynamoDB lock table.
error: the stack is currently locked by another process
Lock Info:
ID: abc-123
Who: ci-runner-pr-441@github
Operation: Updating
Created: 2026-03-12 14:22:11Lock age: 47 minutes. PR job had died after an OOM on a sidecar test container. Lock remained. Nobody had a runbook entry for “stale Pulumi lock” because Terraform had been the previous religion.
Fix: pulumi cancel + documented who may force-unlock (two-person rule). Time lost: ~1.5 hours of platform team attention.
Day 5 — Bun-specific surprise
One npm package used Node’s worker_threads in a way Bun emulated differently. Pulumi component failed at preview with a stack trace through __internal APIs. Not Bun's fault. Not Pulumi's fault. Ecosystem long tail.
Workaround: split that component to a tiny Node runtime stack — hybrid. We wanted pure Bun; we got 95% Bun, which is still a win.
The RDS apply that ignored cold start entirely
Week two we added RDS — db.r6g.large, Multi-AZ, 30GB storage, parameter group tweaks. The apply itself ran ~18 minutes of AWS API waiting. Bun program execution was under 3 minutes of that window. Node wouldn’t have changed the shape.
What hurt:

• pulumi up started from a laptop on hotel Wi-Fi during a conference — lock taken, CI blocked ~52 minutes

• Security group rule typo — RDS reachable from wrong CIDR — caught in preview on retry, not because of runtime

• Secrets Manager version race — two components read the same secret ARN during parallel resource creation
Cold start evangelism misses that IaC time is API time. Optimize human lock behavior first.
We added stack tags for last-successful-apply and wired a Grafana annotation on every green pipeline. Sounds ceremonial. It stopped "who applied last?" arguments in Slack.
Developer experience — the quiet win
Junior engineer quote after week one: “I don’t understand Pulumi less. I understand npm less.” Install failures dropped — fewer node-gyp dramas on Apple Silicon laptops because CI is Linux and Bun's install path was boring.
Type errors still failed in preview. Good. Bun didn’t magically fix bad TypeScript. It removed waiting — and waiting is what makes people skip preview and yolo apply.
Onboarding doc diff:

• Before: install Node 20, npm ci, pulumi login, ~25 minutes first preview

• After: install Bun 1.3.x, bun install, pulumi login, ~11 minutes first preview
Not life-changing. Noticeable in week one of a new hire.
Cold start vs lock vs apply duration
We measured 10 applies on the same stack size (added resources incrementally):
Phase Bun runtime median Previous Node stack (similar size) bun install (cold) 38s npm 130s Program startup → preview 4.2s 6.8s (ts-node path) Preview execution 45–120s 50–130s Lock wait (human) 0–47 min 0–35 min
Cold start was real but noise compared to AWS API rate limits and lock discipline. The 2 AM page was lock age >30 minutes on a production stack apply blocked by a forgotten preview from a laptop.
→ 30 Production Incidents That Cost $10K+ — IaC lock incidents, stale CI holders, and the deploy windows that look green until someone tries to ship.
What we shipped as policy

• One apply lane per stack — preview and up don’t share locks without queue

• Lock age alert at 15 minutes → Slack #infra with holder identity

• CI always pulumi cancel on failure — finally block, not best effort

• Bun runtime for greenfield; Node runtime for packages that touch worker_threads hacks

• No laptop applies to shared prod stacks — CI only, with audit log
Bun saved minutes per pipeline. Policy saved hours per incident.
HCP Terraform envy — and why we stayed on S3 backend
Someone asked why not HCP Terraform for lock UI. Cost for our team size: ~$40/user/month × 6 platform folks vs S3 + DynamoDB pennies. We built lock age dashboard in Grafana for ~3 hours instead. Not as pretty. Good enough.
Drift detection still runs pulumi preview nightly — runtime choice didn't change drift. Bun preview was ~15% faster on empty diff nights. On 400-resource stacks with real drift, AWS API dominated again.
Importing brownfield without runtime regret
Week four we imported an existing VPC — ~80 resources — from a client handoff. Import path under Bun:

• pulumi import batch script: ~2.4 hours wall clock

• Human time fixing wrong IDs: ~6 hours (runtime irrelevant)

• One import failed because provider version pinned differently between stacks — Bun and Node would have failed identically
Takeaway: runtime: bun doesn’t shorten import archaeology. It shortens the loops while you’re fixing IDs. When you’re clicking through AWS console at 1 AM, every saved 30 seconds on preview adds up.
We kept Bun for the greenfield stack. Client import stack stayed Node until provider parity was proven — two runtimes, one team, documented in README. Pragmatism over purity.
CI caching — the other half of the Bun story
Beyond bun install, we cached:

• .pulumi/ plugin binaries per provider version

• Bun’s global cache tarball in GitHub Actions (~190MB hit, ~12MB miss)
Second-week pipeline median: ~4m 20s preview vs ~7m 10s on old Node pipeline for same diff size. Not all Bun — caching matured same sprint. Split the win honestly in retros or you’ll defend the wrong lever next budget cycle.
Policy test: we blocked a Friday 17:00 apply with a failing preview — Bun startup made the failure feel “faster,” which almost made the team ignore it. Speed without discipline is just faster yolo.
When runtime: bun is worth it

• Greenfield TypeScript IaC

• Teams already standardized on Bun locally

• Pipelines where install + startup are measurable line items
When we keep Node

• Heavy provider SDK edge cases

• Function serialization features Pulumi documents as Node-only

• Teams without appetite for runtime churn
What you should do this week

• If you adopt runtime: bun, run one full apply on a throwaway stack before the production import.

• Add lock age monitoring before you optimize install time.

• Document cancel / unlock with names, not heroes.

• Keep a Node escape hatch for one component if ecosystem bites.

• Time end-to-end apply, not just pulumi preview.
Fast runtime doesn’t fix slow operations culture.
For the infra side when the apply turns into an incident — → Production Incident War Room — minute-by-minute first response when CI is red and the lock holder is a dead pod.
Froquiz covers AWS, Docker, Git, and scenario-based infra judgment calls — built for the 2 AM apply, not the tutorial.
→ Froquiz
